loan.def: Money that is borrowed and must be paid back
ex: They took out a loan to buy the house.
Student loans can take years to pay off.

lobby.def: To seek to influence a politician on an issue
ex: Environmental groups lobbied the government to ban plastic bags.
The industry has been lobbying against stricter regulations.
What a lovely day!
She wore a lovely dress to the wedding.

magnify.def: To make something appear larger or more important than it is
ex: The media tends to magnify minor disagreements between politicians.
A microscope magnifies objects up to a thousand times their actual size.

maintain.def: To keep something in good condition or continue doing it
ex: It's important to maintain a healthy lifestyle.
The building is well maintained.

majority.def: More than half of a group
ex: The majority of students passed the exam.
The bill was passed by a large majority.

manage.def: To succeed in doing something difficult
ex: I managed to finish the report on time.
How do you manage to stay so calm?

manifest.def: To display or show a quality or feeling clearly
ex: The illness can manifest itself in many different ways.
His frustration manifested as anger toward his colleagues.

manipulate.def: To control or influence someone or something cleverly or unscrupulously
ex: He accused the media of manipulating public opinion.
The data had been manipulated to show better results.

matter.def: A subject or situation being considered
ex: It's a personal matter.
What's the matter? You look upset.

measure.def: To find the size or amount of something
ex: Can you measure the width of the room?
Success is difficult to measure.

media.def: Television, newspapers, and the internet as a group
ex: The media reported the story immediately.
Social media has changed how we communicate.

medieval.def: Relating to the Middle Ages (roughly 5th to 15th century)
ex: The town has a beautiful medieval castle.
Medieval manuscripts reveal much about daily life in that era.

memoir.def: A written account of someone's personal experiences
ex: Her memoir about growing up in poverty became a bestseller.
He published his memoirs after retiring from politics.
She mentioned that she was moving to a new city.
Did he mention the meeting?

merit.def: The quality of being particularly good or worthy
ex: Each case should be judged on its own merit.
The proposal has considerable merit.

method.def: A particular way of doing something
ex: What method did you use to solve the problem?
There are several methods of payment available.

mitigate.def: To make something less severe, serious, or painful
ex: Measures were taken to mitigate the impact of the floods.
Nothing could mitigate the grief she felt after the loss.
Mix the flour and eggs in a bowl.
You should not mix work and personal life.

momentum.def: The force gained by a moving object; impetus of progress
ex: The campaign is gaining momentum ahead of the election.
Once the reforms started, they quickly built momentum.

monopoly.def: Exclusive control of the supply of a commodity or service
ex: The company held a virtual monopoly on the market.
The government broke up the telecommunications monopoly.

moreover.def: In addition to what has been said
ex: The hotel is cheap. Moreover, it's close to the beach.
He's experienced and, moreover, very creative.

mortality.def: The state of being subject to death; the death rate
ex: Infant mortality has decreased significantly in recent decades.
The disease has a high mortality rate if left untreated.

motivation.def: The reason or desire to do something
ex: Money is not my only motivation.
Students need motivation to study hard.

municipal.def: Relating to a city or town and its local government
ex: The municipal council approved the new housing plan.
Recycling is managed by the municipal waste department.

murder.def: The crime of killing someone on purpose
ex: The police are investigating a murder.
He was arrested for murder.
Her handwriting is very neat.
Keep your desk neat and clean.

negotiate.def: To discuss something to reach an agreement
ex: They negotiated a better salary.
The two countries are negotiating a peace deal.
She felt nervous before the interview.
I always get nervous before exams.

nevertheless.def: In spite of what has just been said
ex: It was raining; nevertheless, we went for a walk.
The task was difficult. Nevertheless, she completed it on time.

niche.def: A specialized segment of the market; a comfortable or suitable position
ex: The company found its niche in organic cosmetics.
She carved out a niche for herself in academic publishing.

nominal.def: Existing in name only; very small in amount
ex: They charge only a nominal fee for the service.
He held the nominal title of director but had no real power.
The city is notorious for its traffic jams.
He was a notorious criminal who evaded police for years.

nowadays.def: At the present time, in contrast to the past
ex: Nowadays, everyone has a smartphone.
People travel more nowadays than before.

nurture.def: To care for and encourage the growth of someone or something
ex: Parents must nurture their children's natural curiosity.
The program nurtures young talent in the arts.

oblivious.def: Not aware of or not concerned about what is happening around one
ex: She seemed oblivious to the danger she was in.
Lost in thought, he was oblivious to the passing of time.

observe.def: To watch carefully or notice something
ex: Scientists observed the behavior of the animals.
I observed that she seemed very tired.
It was obvious that she was lying.
The answer is obvious if you think about it.
The accident occurred early in the morning.
It never occurred to me to check the back door.
The government's decision-making process remains opaque to outsiders.
The windows were made of opaque glass for privacy.

opportunity.def: A chance to do something good
ex: This job is a great opportunity for you.
Don't miss the opportunity to travel abroad.

opt.def: To make a choice from a range of possibilities
ex: Many students opt for online courses these days.
She opted to stay home rather than go to the party.
It was just an ordinary day.
She is no ordinary student — she is brilliant.

ought.def: Should, used to say what is right to do
ex: You ought to apologize to her.
We ought to leave before it gets dark.

outweigh.def: To be greater or more significant than something else
ex: The benefits of exercise far outweigh the risks.
His experience outweighed his lack of formal qualifications.
The overall cost was higher than expected.
Overall, I'm satisfied with the results.

overt.def: Done or shown openly; not secret or hidden
ex: There was no overt hostility, but tension was palpable.
The regime engaged in overt censorship of the press.

paradigm.def: A typical example or pattern; a worldview underlying theories
ex: The internet created a new paradigm in communication.
The discovery led to a paradigm shift in physics.

paradox.def: A statement or situation that seems contradictory but may be true
ex: It's a paradox that the richest countries have the most debt.
The paradox of choice is that more options can lead to less satisfaction.

parity.def: The state of being equal, especially in status or pay
ex: The union demanded pay parity with workers in similar industries.
Gender parity in leadership positions remains an elusive goal.

participate.def: To take part in an activity
ex: Everyone is encouraged to participate in the discussion.
She participated in the marathon last year.

patient.def: Able to wait calmly without getting angry
ex: Please be patient — the doctor will see you soon.
She is very patient with her students.

patron.def: A person who supports an organization or artist; a regular customer
ex: The gallery depends on wealthy patrons for funding.
Patrons of the library can borrow up to ten books.

peculiar.def: Strange or unusual; belonging exclusively to a particular person or place
ex: There was a peculiar smell coming from the basement.
This custom is peculiar to the northern regions.

perform.def: To do something, especially in front of people
ex: The band performed at the festival.
The surgeon performed the operation successfully.
Stereotypes in the media perpetuate harmful misconceptions.
The scholarship was established to perpetuate his legacy.

pertinent.def: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter
ex: The lawyer raised several pertinent questions during cross-examination.
Only include information that is pertinent to the discussion.

petition.def: A formal written request signed by many people appealing to an authority
ex: Over 10,000 people signed the online petition.
They filed a petition with the court to overturn the decision.
His excuse wasbarely plausible.
There is no plausible alternative explanation for the data.
We had a pleasant evening together.
The weather is quite pleasant today.

pledge.def: To commit formally to give or do something
ex: The president pledged to reduce unemployment.
Donors pledged over two million dollars to the relief effort.
There is plenty of food for everyone.
We have plenty of time before the train leaves.

plunge.def: To fall or move suddenly downward or into something
ex: Share prices plunged after the announcement.
He plunged into the icy water without hesitation.
He is always polite to strangers.
It is polite to say thank you.

pollution.def: Damage caused to air, water, or land by harmful substances
ex: Air pollution is a big problem in cities.
The factory caused pollution in the river.

portray.def: To depict or describe someone or something in a particular way
ex: The film portrays life in a small fishing village.
The media often portrays teenagers negatively.

postpone.def: To arrange for something to take place at a later time
ex: The match was postponed due to heavy rain.
They decided to postpone the wedding until next spring.
There are potential risks with this approach.
She has the potential to become a great leader.

pour.def: To make liquid flow from a container
ex: She poured water into the glass.
It was pouring with rain all day.

poverty.def: The state of being very poor
ex: Millions of people live in poverty.
The government is trying to reduce poverty.

practitioner.def: A person actively engaged in a profession or activity
ex: She is a medical practitioner with 20 years of experience.
Legal practitioners must stay updated on new legislation.

precedent.def: An earlier event or action regarded as an example for later situations
ex: The ruling set a legal precedent for future cases.
There is no precedent for a decision of this magnitude.
Can you give me the precise time of the meeting?
She gave a very precise description of the thief.

preclude.def: To prevent something from happening; to make impossible
ex: His injury precluded him from competing in the Olympics.
The terms of the agreement preclude any further legal action.

pretend.def: To act as if something is true when it is not
ex: He pretended to be asleep.
Don't pretend you didn't hear me.

prevalent.def: Widespread in a particular area or at a particular time
ex: Corruption is prevalent in many developing countries.
The disease is most prevalent among children under five.
Wearing a seatbelt can prevent injuries.
We must prevent this from happening again.

principle.def: A basic rule or belief that guides behavior
ex: He's a man of strong principles.
The principle behind this method is simple.

procure.def: To obtain something, especially with care or effort
ex: The agency struggled to procure enough vaccines for the population.
He was charged with procuring false documents.

profound.def: Very great or intense; having deep meaning
ex: The war had a profound effect on the entire generation.
His words left a profound impression on the audience.

promote.def: To raise someone to a higher position or support something
ex: She was promoted to manager last month.
The campaign promotes healthy eating.
The committee rejected the proposal.
He made a proposal to improve working conditions.

proud.def: Feeling pleased about something you or someone else has done
ex: She is proud of her children.
I am proud to be part of this team.

prove.def: To show that something is true
ex: Can you prove that you were at home?
The test results proved he was right.

provoke.def: To stimulate a strong reaction or emotion, often anger
ex: His comments provoked an angry response from the opposition.
The article was designed to provoke debate.
The house's proximity to the school was a key selling point.
The proximity of the deadline forced them to work overtime.

publish.def: To make a book, article, or information available to the public
ex: She published her first novel last year.
The results will be published next week.

punish.def: To make someone suffer for doing something wrong
ex: The teacher punished the student for cheating.
He was punished for breaking the rules.

react.def: To behave in a particular way because of something
ex: How did she react to the news?
He reacted angrily when he heard the decision.

realize.def: To understand something you did not know before
ex: I realized I had left my phone at home.
She suddenly realized the truth.

realm.def: A field or domain of activity or interest; a kingdom
ex: She is an expert in the realm of artificial intelligence.
His ideas moved from the realm of theory into practice.

recommend.def: To suggest that something is good or suitable
ex: I recommend this book — it is great.
The doctor recommended more exercise.

reconcile.def: To restore friendly relations; to make compatible
ex: The couple tried to reconcile after their separation.
It's hard to reconcile his public image with his private behavior.

recover.def: To get better after an illness or difficult time
ex: She's recovering from surgery.
The economy is slowly recovering.

reduce.def: To make something smaller or less
ex: We need to reduce our spending.
The shop reduced prices by 20 percent.

reflect.def: To think carefully about something or show an image
ex: Take some time to reflect on your goals.
The lake reflected the mountains perfectly.

refrain.def: To stop oneself from doing something
ex: Please refrain from smoking inside the building.
She refrained from making any comments to the press.
She refused to answer the question.
He refused the offer politely.

register.def: To put your name on an official list
ex: You need to register for the course online.
Over 200 people registered for the event.

regret.def: To feel sorry about something you did or did not do
ex: I regret not studying harder at school.
She regretted her decision immediately.

relevant.def: Connected to what is being discussed
ex: Please include all relevant information.
His experience is highly relevant to this job.
Please remind me to call the dentist.
This song reminds me of my childhood.

repair.def: To fix something that is broken
ex: He repaired the broken chair.
How much will it cost to repair my phone?

repercussion.def: An unintended consequence of an action, especially an unwelcome one
ex: The policy change had serious repercussions for small businesses.
He acted without thinking about the repercussions.

replace.def: To put something new in place of something old
ex: We need to replace the old batteries.
Nobody can replace a good friend.

represent.def: To act or speak officially for someone
ex: A lawyer will represent you in court.
This painting represents freedom.
She resigned from her position last week.
He resigned because of health problems.

resource.def: Something useful that is available to use
ex: Water is our most precious natural resource.
The library has many online resources.

respect.def: A feeling of admiration for someone
ex: Children should show respect to their elders.
I have great respect for her work.

responsibility.def: A duty to deal with something
ex: It is your responsibility to lock the door.
She took responsibility for the mistake.

retaliate.def: To make an attack in return for a similar attack
ex: The country threatened to retaliate with economic sanctions.
She resisted the urge to retaliate against her critics.

rob.def: To steal from a person or place
ex: Someone robbed the bank last night.
He was robbed on his way home.
The sea was very rough during the storm.
The surface of the road is rough.

salary.def: The money you receive every month for your work
ex: His salary is not enough to pay the rent.
She got a salary increase this year.

satisfy.def: To make someone pleased by giving them what they need
ex: The answer did not satisfy her.
Nothing satisfies him — he always wants more.
The loud noise scared the children.
Don't scare me like that!

scrutinize.def: To examine something very carefully and critically
ex: Auditors scrutinized the company's financial records.
Every aspect of his private life was scrutinized by the press.

secret.def: Something that is kept hidden from others
ex: Can you keep a secret?
She told me all her secrets.

select.def: To choose something from a group
ex: Please select your preferred language.
He was selected for the national team.
The children sleep in separate rooms.
Keep your personal life separate from work.
She had a severe headache.
The country faced severe flooding.

shall.def: Used to make suggestions or offers
ex: Shall we go for a walk?
Shall I open the window?

shame.def: A painful feeling caused by doing something wrong
ex: He felt shame after lying to his parents.
What a shame you cannot come to the party.

sharp.def: Having a thin edge that can cut
ex: Be careful — that knife is very sharp.
The cat has sharp claws.

significant.def: Important or large enough to have an effect
ex: There has been a significant improvement.
The difference in price is not significant.
There was silence in the room after he spoke.
She sat in silence for a long time.
Don't ask silly questions.
It was a silly mistake.

site.def: A place where something is or happens
ex: The company is building on a new site.
This is the site of an ancient temple.

smooth.def: Having an even surface without bumps
ex: The baby has very smooth skin.
The road was smooth and easy to drive on.

solicit.def: To ask for something from someone in a formal way
ex: The organization solicited donations from local businesses.
The committee solicited feedback from all stakeholders before deciding.
What is your main source of income?
The internet is a great source of information.

steal.def: To take something that belongs to someone else
ex: Someone stole my wallet on the bus.
It is wrong to steal.

storm.def: Very bad weather with strong wind and rain
ex: A terrible storm hit the city last night.
The storm knocked down several trees.

strategy.def: A plan designed to achieve a goal
ex: We need a new marketing strategy.
What's your strategy for passing the exam?
The country imposed stringent regulations on carbon emissions.
Stringent quality controls ensure product safety.
She succeeded in passing all her exams.
If you keep trying, you will succeed.

succumb.def: To fail to resist pressure, temptation, or a disease
ex: Many patients succumbed to the illness before a cure was found.
After months of pressure, the CEO succumbed and resigned.

suppress.def: To forcibly put an end to something; to prevent from being expressed
ex: The regime suppressed all forms of political dissent.
She struggled to suppress a smile during the serious meeting.

surmount.def: To overcome a difficulty or obstacle
ex: With determination, she surmounted every challenge in her career.
The technical obstacles were difficult but not impossible to surmount.

target.def: A goal or result you are trying to achieve
ex: We met our sales target this quarter.
The target audience for this product is young adults.

temperature.def: How hot or cold something is
ex: The temperature dropped to minus five last night.
What is the temperature outside?
The theme of the story is love and loss.
The party had a space theme.
She cut the bread into thin slices.
He is tall and thin.

throat.def: The front part of your neck, or the passage inside
ex: I have a sore throat.
The food got stuck in his throat.
These shoes are too tight.
Hold on tight — the bus is about to move.

tongue.def: The soft part inside your mouth that you use for tasting
ex: She burned her tongue on the hot soup.
The word was on the tip of my tongue.

tough.def: Difficult, or strong and not easily broken
ex: It was a tough decision to make.
You need to be tough to survive in this job.

tradition.def: A custom that has existed for a long time
ex: It is a tradition to eat turkey on Thanksgiving.
We should keep our traditions alive.

translate.def: To change words from one language to another
ex: Can you translate this sentence into Turkish?
She translates books for a living.

treat.def: To behave towards someone in a particular way
ex: You should treat people with kindness.
The doctor treated his injury.

trick.def: Something done to fool or surprise someone
ex: The magician showed us an amazing trick.
It was just a trick — nobody was hurt.
The building is ugly but functional.
She said some ugly things during the argument.
The decision ultimately depends on you.
They ultimately agreed to the terms.

unfortunately.def: Used to say that something is sad or disappointing
ex: Unfortunately, the shop was closed.
Unfortunately, I cannot come to your party.
The team united to solve the problem.
Music can unite people from different cultures.
She was upset when she heard the news.
Don't get upset — we can fix this.

valley.def: A low area of land between hills or mountains
ex: The village is in a beautiful green valley.
A river runs through the valley.
The shop sells a wide variety of cheese.
Variety is the spice of life.

vindicate.def: To clear someone of blame or show that something is justified
ex: The court ruling vindicated her after years of false accusations.
Recent discoveries have vindicated the scientist's controversial theory.

warn.def: To tell someone about a possible danger
ex: I warned you not to go there alone.
The sign warns drivers about the sharp turn.

wealth.def: A large amount of money or possessions
ex: His family has great wealth.
Health is more important than wealth.

weigh.def: To measure how heavy something is
ex: How much do you weigh?
She weighed the fruit before paying.
This painting is worth a million dollars.
The film is definitely worth watching.
